# AdaptTrap -- Evaluation Report

*Auto-generated by `evaluation/report_generator.py`. Checkpoint: `logs/model_best2.pt`. Seeds: [42, 123].*

## Setup

- Attacker tiers: recon_probe, scripted_exploit, ai_probe
- Defenders compared: static, random, rule_based, rl_greedy
- Episodes per attacker per run: 50 (150 episodes/defender/run)
- Independent seeds: 42 and 123
- Checkpoint evaluated: `logs/model_best2.pt` (same checkpoint, both runs)


## Headline result

RL defender beats every non-RL baseline on raw reward, in both independent seed runs, on every attacker tier:

| Attacker | Seed | Baseline (static) reward | RL reward | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| recon_probe | 42 | 13.6258 | 14.9675 | +9.85% |
| recon_probe | 123 | 13.5669 | 14.9775 | +10.40% |
| scripted_exploit | 42 | 19.3385 | 20.9362 | +8.26% |
| scripted_exploit | 123 | 19.274 | 20.915 | +8.51% |
| ai_probe | 42 | 34.4862 | 41.2459 | +19.60% |
| ai_probe | 123 | 33.3026 | 41.2703 | +23.93% |

![Reward by attacker](logs/plots/reward_by_attacker.png)

### Detection rate against `ai_probe` (hardest tier)

Zero observed detections is not the same claim as a zero true detection rate. Two units of analysis are reported below. **Episode-level is the primary, defensible claim** -- within a single episode the defender observes `session_count` and `consec_detects` (see `HoneypotEnv._get_obs()`), so its action in session *t* can depend on the outcome of sessions *1..t-1*. That breaks strict session-to-session independence on the defender side, even though each attacker instance itself is freshly built with no carried state (`HoneypotEnv._build_attacker()`):

- **Episode-level (primary claim)** -- one independent trial per full 20-session episode; counts as a detection if any session inside it was flagged: 0.00% observed (n=100, 95% CI: [0.000%, 3.622%])

- **Session-level (descriptive telemetry only -- not used for the confidence interval)**: 0 of 2000 individual sessions flagged. Reported as a raw count, not as an independent-trial rate, because the defender-side dependency described above applies at this unit of analysis.

Same comparison for the non-RL baselines against `ai_probe`, pooled across both seeds (episode-level, the primary unit):

| Defender | Episodes with >=1 detection | Episode-level rate (95% CI) |
|---|---|---|
| static | 70 / 100 | 70.00% observed (n=100, 95% CI: [60.019%, 78.759%]) |
| random | 100 / 100 | 100.00% observed (n=100, 95% CI: [96.378%, 100.000%]) |
| rule_based | 99 / 100 | 99.00% observed (n=100, 95% CI: [94.554%, 99.975%]) |
| rl_greedy | 0 / 100 | 0.00% observed (n=100, 95% CI: [0.000%, 3.622%]) |

![Detection rate by attacker](logs/plots/detection_rate_by_attacker.png)

![Dwell time by attacker](logs/plots/dwell_time_by_attacker.png)

## Training curve

1500-episode training run, curriculum phases marked. See `logs/training2.json` for the full per-episode log.

![Training reward curve](logs/plots/training_reward_curve.png)


> **Known artifact -- `normalized_reward` is not a comparison metric.**
> `mean_total_normalized_reward` in the raw eval JSONs is a per-run,
> self-referential rolling z-score: `HoneypotEnv` builds a fresh reward
> history per (defender, attacker) run and normalizes each step against
> *that run's own* mean/std. Because a consistently strong policy sits close
> to its own running mean, its normalized score compresses toward zero even
> though its raw reward is higher -- this is why `rl_greedy` shows a *lower*
> normalized reward than `static` despite winning on every raw metric. This
> is a reporting-layer artifact, not a policy deficiency. **`raw_reward` and
> `detection_rate` are the metrics this report treats as ground truth.**


## Scope and limitations
 
- Single simulated environment (SSH/HTTP/Redis emulated services), not a
  live network target.
- Three attacker tiers, two of which (`recon_probe`, `scripted_exploit`)
  are scripted/heuristic rather than adaptive; only `ai_probe` learns within
  a session (fresh Q-learning bandit instance per session -- see
  `attackers/level3_bandit.py`).
- No attacker in this benchmark adapts *to the trained RL policy itself*
  across sessions or episodes -- each attacker session starts from a fresh
  instance with no memory of prior defender behavior. The results show the
  defender beating fixed/heuristic adversaries, not an arms-race outcome
  against an adversary that is itself learning to counter the RL policy.
- Evaluated on CPU, `fast_training=True` timing scale, 2 random seeds
  (42, 123), 50 episodes per attacker per seed.
